name: new_err_valid_input
reason: The spec never mandates Ok for valid inputs; a trivially-rejecting implementation that always returns Err would satisfy all ensures clauses, revealing a liveness gap.

name: new_err_valid_input_minimal
reason: Even the smallest valid bitmap size (8 bits = 1 byte) can be rejected under this spec, confirming the liveness gap at the lower boundary.

name: new_err_valid_input_typical
reason: A common bitmap size (256 bits = 32 bytes) is not guaranteed to succeed, confirming the spec gap is not limited to edge cases.

name: new_err_valid_input_near_max
reason: The upper boundary of valid inputs is not guaranteed to produce Ok, confirming the liveness gap extends to the maximum valid bitmap size.

name: alloc_always_err
reason: If verified, the spec would allow an implementation that unconditionally returns Err, violating the liveness expectation that alloc succeeds when free bits exist.

name: alloc_always_err_empty
reason: An empty bitmap (usage==0) has all bits free; returning Err here contradicts is_full() since num_bits > 0 from inv(), so the spec should reject this.

name: alloc_always_err_almost_full
reason: With exactly one free bit (usage == num_bits - 1), alloc should succeed; returning Err contradicts is_full() since usage < num_bits.

name: alloc_always_err_half_full
reason: With half the bits free (usage == num_bits/2), alloc should succeed; returning Err contradicts is_full() since usage < num_bits.

name: alloc_always_zero
reason: The spec allows always returning index 0 when it is free, causing starvation of higher-indexed bits.

name: alloc_always_zero_empty
reason: On an empty bitmap every bit is free; returning 0 is trivially valid, so the spec cannot prevent always choosing 0.

name: alloc_always_zero_others_free
reason: Multiple bits are free but alloc always picks bit 0; the spec has no fairness requirement preventing this.

name: alloc_always_zero_bit0_set
reason: Control case: when bit 0 is already set, returning 0 violates the precondition !old(self)@.is_bit_set(index). Verus should reject this, confirming the spec blocks this specific abuse.

name: alloc_flips_other_bits
reason: If the frame condition for non-allocated bits is missing, alloc could corrupt other bitmap entries while correctly setting the target bit.

name: alloc_flips_other_bits_empty
reason: On an empty bitmap no bits are set, but alloc could spuriously set extra bits besides the allocated one—losing free slots silently.

name: alloc_flips_other_bits_in_use
reason: Clearing a previously-set bit means an allocated resource is silently freed, causing a use-after-free or double-allocation bug.

name: alloc_flips_other_bits_almost_full
reason: When almost full, clearing any set bit creates a spurious free slot, allowing double allocation of the same resource.

name: set_err_valid_clear
reason: The spec never forces Ok when index is valid and the bit is clear; a trivially-rejecting implementation satisfies all ensures vacuously for the Ok branch.

name: set_err_valid_clear_index0
reason: Simplest valid case (index 0, bit clear); spec allows Err since the Ok branch is conditional and never mandated.

name: set_err_valid_clear_last_index
reason: Boundary index (last valid bit, clear); spec allows Err at the boundary just as easily as interior indices since the Ok branch is never forced.

name: set_err_valid_clear_already_set
reason: Control case: bit is already set, so Err is the correct behavior per implementation; this proof fn SHOULD verify, confirming the spec correctly allows Err when the bit is already set.

name: clear_already_cleared_ok
reason: If the spec allows Ok when the bit is already clear, usage would be decremented incorrectly (underflow) and set_bits.remove would be a no-op mismatch, corrupting bitmap accounting.

name: clear_already_cleared_ok_bit_clear
reason: The Err branch's condition `!old(self)@.is_bit_set(index)` should force Err when the bit is already clear, but the Ok ensures clause lacks `old(self)@.is_bit_set(index)` — if entailed, it means usage underflow is permitted.

name: clear_already_cleared_ok_bit_set
reason: Baseline control: when the bit IS set, the spec should allow Ok — if this also fails, the spec may be overly restrictive rather than incomplete.

name: clear_already_cleared_ok_all_clear
reason: When usage is 0 and all bits are clear, allowing Ok would force usage to become -1 (underflow), a severe accounting error; this tests whether the spec's ensures clause catches this extreme case.

name: alloc_range_err_despite_free_space
reason: If the spec allows Err universally, alloc_range is useless even when free space exists — a basic liveness violation.

name: alloc_range_err_despite_free_space_entire_empty
reason: A completely empty bitmap with size == num_bits has the full contiguous range [0, num_bits) free; returning Err here is a boundary-case liveness violation.

name: alloc_range_err_despite_free_space_minimal_empty
reason: A size-1 request on a fully-empty bitmap is the simplest possible allocation; Err here means the allocator rejects even trivially satisfiable requests.

name: alloc_range_err_despite_free_space_exact_fit
reason: When exactly one contiguous free region of the right size remains, returning Err wastes the last available space — a critical boundary failure for tight allocations.

name: from_raw_array_vacuous_err
reason: The ensures has no Err branch, so an Err return under valid preconditions is unconstrained—the spec claims liveness but the encoding may not enforce it

name: from_raw_array_vacuous_err_minimal
reason: Even the simplest valid input (1-byte array = 8 bits) could return Err with the ensures vacuously satisfied, if the encoding treats `matches Ok(_) && P` as conditional

name: from_raw_array_vacuous_err_near_max
reason: Near-maximum arrays are closest to the checked_mul overflow boundary in the implementation; if the spec vacuously accepts Err here, callers at scale get no guarantees

name: from_raw_array_vacuous_err_all_zeros
reason: Even with a concretely all-zero array that trivially satisfies every precondition, the ensures may vacuously pass for Err, showing the spec lacks an explicit liveness clause

name: alloc_range_nondeterministic
reason: The spec permits choosing any valid free range, so allocation order is non-deterministic from a caller's perspective — the body's next-fit policy is unspecified.

name: alloc_range_nondeterministic_disjoint
reason: With two disjoint free ranges, the spec permits choosing the higher-index one, violating the body's first-fit-from-zero scan order.

name: alloc_range_nondeterministic_before_after_nextfree
reason: The spec permits returning a range before next_free, contradicting the body's circular scan that checks after next_free first.

name: alloc_range_nondeterministic_wraparound
reason: After wrapping around, the body finds the first free range in scan order, but the spec allows any valid free range — even one the scan would pass second.

name: next_free_cursor_not_specified
reason: The spec allows non-adjacent successive allocations even when adjacent free space exists, because `next_free` cursor advancement is unspecified.

name: next_free_cursor_not_specified_middle
reason: After allocating from the middle, the body advances next_free past the block, but the spec allows the next alloc to return a position before it, violating next-fit locality.

name: next_free_cursor_not_specified_near_end
reason: After allocating at the end, next_free reaches num_bits and should wrap to 0 on the next call, but the spec allows any valid free range, not necessarily the one found by wrapping.

name: next_free_cursor_not_specified_sequential
reason: On a mostly-empty bitmap, the body's next-fit guarantees contiguous sequential allocations, but the spec permits gaps because next_free advancement is unspecified.

name: clear_no_next_free_update
reason: Without next_free update in the spec, callers cannot rely on freed low-index slots being reused by subsequent alloc calls.

name: clear_no_next_free_update_below_cursor
reason: Callers clearing a bit below the allocation cursor cannot prove that subsequent alloc will find the freed slot before higher-index slots.

name: clear_no_next_free_update_at_or_above
reason: Confirms the spec gap is asymmetric — the missing constraint only matters when index < next_free, not when index >= next_free.

name: clear_no_next_free_update_lowest_set
reason: Freeing the lowest-indexed occupied slot should guarantee it is reused next, but without the cursor constraint in the spec, callers cannot prove this.

name: new_liveness
reason: The spec permits returning Err for any valid input; a trivially-rejecting implementation satisfies all ensures clauses, revealing a liveness gap absent from the analogous from_raw_array spec.

name: new_liveness_memory_exhausted
reason: The body reveals RawArray::new(len)? can fail on allocation, but the spec makes no liveness promise even for feasible allocations — a caller cannot distinguish allocation failure from a spurious rejection.

name: new_liveness_small_valid
reason: Even the smallest valid bitmap (1 byte) is not guaranteed to succeed under this spec; verified code cannot depend on new(8) returning Ok, unlike from_raw_array which provides explicit liveness.

name: stale_cursor
reason: If entailed, the spec allows set to succeed without constraining the allocation cursor, making subsequent alloc behavior unpredictable

name: stale_cursor_at_next_free
reason: If entailed, setting the bit at the cursor position leaves next_free pointing to an occupied bit, causing alloc to waste cycles scanning past it

name: stale_cursor_after_alloc_advance
reason: If entailed, setting a bit behind the advanced cursor is invisible to alloc until wrap-around, causing free bits before next_free to be skipped

name: stale_cursor_alternating
reason: If entailed, interleaving set and alloc leaves the cursor inconsistent with actual free-bit positions, making alloc placement unpredictable across the alternation pattern

name: alloc_range_capacity_insufficient_implies_err
reason: If the spec allows Ok when usage > num_bits - size, it contradicts the pigeonhole principle — the body correctly rejects this case via an O(1) check, but the spec doesn't independently express this sufficient condition for failure.

name: alloc_range_capacity_insufficient_implies_err_full
reason: A completely full bitmap (usage == num_bits) has zero free bits; any allocation must fail. If the spec allows Ok here, it violates basic capacity reasoning the body enforces via its O(1) check.

name: alloc_range_capacity_insufficient_implies_err_one_short
reason: With only (size - 1) free bits total, no contiguous range of `size` can exist by the pigeonhole principle. The body catches this at O(1) cost; the spec should independently express that insufficient total free bits precludes allocation success.

name: alloc_range_capacity_insufficient_implies_err_fragmented
reason: When capacity is sufficient but bits are fragmented, the body does a full O(n) scan before returning Err. The spec conflates this with the O(1) capacity-exceeded path — a caller cannot distinguish "not enough total bits" from "enough bits but fragmented", which matters for deciding whether compaction/defragmentation could help.
